# Questions in Livestream 248

Live Stream #248: An Angel Under Every Rock

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=842" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:02</a> | Were the Egyptian gods regarded as corporeal beings or as spirit beings? | He said the Egyptians treated them as both: they had real embodied scope within creation, but were not reduced to merely physical creatures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:25</a> | Does humanity being made in God's image mean God is corporeal and physically resembles us? | No. He treated that as an equivocation fallacy and said `image of God` does not require a physical resemblance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=1063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:43</a> | Why does Ezekiel 16:3 single out the Amorite father and Hittite mother of Jerusalem? | He connected the language to Israel's origins in the broader Aramean-Hittite world tied to Terah's line, rather than to every earlier Canaanite group equally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=1351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:31</a> | Why is Amos 9:7 not seen as clear proof for the Exodus if it gets other peoples' migrations right? | He said it strains credulity to accept the Philistine and Aramean origins but dismiss Israel's own origin, and framed many skeptical readings as carrying a heavy cognitive load. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=1537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:37</a> | Why did Joshua not reuse the captured chariots in Joshua 11:6-9? | He leaned toward a practical and theological reading together: chariots were not useful for Israel's setting, and disabling them underscored reliance on God rather than prestige weaponry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=1806" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:06</a> | Can bad charioting get the driver killed? | Yes. He said chariot training had many fatalities because a bad turn could throw and crush the driver. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=1855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:55</a> | Would you rather be a charioteer or a foot soldier? | A charioteer with training, because of the status and advantages; without training, he would choose foot soldier because chariots were dangerous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=1994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:14</a> | What was the penalty for horse theft in Egypt? | Death, because horses were state property; stealing the king's property carried the death penalty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=2107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:07</a> | How should the church teach the different applications of love? | He grounded love in obedience to Christ, love of neighbor, and practical care, arguing that love must be taught as visible action rather than sentiment alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=2258" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:38</a> | Do we live in a culture that treats symptoms of problems but not root causes? | Not simply. He said society often tries to address causes, but does so through untested theories that can make problems worse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=2599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:19</a> | Do you have a favorite way to season tomato sauce? | Salt, oregano, basil, sometimes olive oil, and Parmesan rind for pasta sauce; he warned against making the sauce sweet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=2833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:13</a> | Is Jesus more accurately understood as the love humanity seeks or the hope humanity desperately needs? | He chose hope, saying humanity was not seeking God's love but needed salvation, and Christ exposes sin as the light. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=3000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:00</a> | Would spirit critters enjoy being petted, or would that be condescending? | He treated it lightly and said it would be dangerous; some might accept or condescend to it, but caution would be wise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=3256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:16</a> | How does the topic of spiritual beings in the Bible relate to daimons? | He said `daimon` is a broader Greek category for spirit beings, while the New Testament uses demons as a catch-all for evil spirits; angels or demons could fall under the wider daimon category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=3453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:33</a> | Have you read Brian Wendell's new book arguing for an Israelite conquest in Late Bronze IIA? | No. He said Wendell mishandles the Hazor data and is constrained by ABR's early-date expectations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=3589" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:49</a> | Was there a power struggle after the death of Amenhotep II? | Probably. He said some struggle seems likely, but the timing and details remain uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=3742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:22</a> | Do you agree that Judges 5:19 implies Iron Age I Megiddo had already been destroyed and that Deborah and Barak's victory may connect to the fall of Iron Age I Taanach? | He was open to Megiddo already being destroyed, but warned that Judges 5 names fighting near Taanach and does not say that any city was destroyed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=3911" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:11</a> | Does an amoral Sheol contradict a later moral afterlife? | He said it would not fit neatly with a later moral-afterlife scheme, but stressed that Sheol is amoral in the sense of being impartial, not immoral. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=4059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:39</a> | Was Sheol ever supposed to be the final place in Jewish understanding? | It depends on the period; by the end of the Second Temple period many Jews expected resurrection from Sheol, so it was not final in that framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=4141" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:01</a> | What does suffering in the afterlife mean: demons torturing humans, or something else? | He rejected the idea of demons torturing people, saying demons suffer too; the misery is more about judgment, loss, and the condition of the condemned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=4293" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:33</a> | Could people in hell hurt each other? | Yes, in his view embodied people could inflict pain, but it would increase misery and eventually give way to apathy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=4488" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:48</a> | Can people leave hell like C.S. Lewis seemed to think? | He said Lewis was not a universalist; Lewis's point was that even if the gates were open, no one in hell would choose holiness and leave. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:41</a> | Is there any truth to stories about the Canadian Army committing war crimes? | He said yes, especially before later rules existed, giving examples such as shooting prisoners and using food and grenades deceptively. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:55</a> | Is the best location for Tarshish in southern Spain, meaning Jonah wanted to go the opposite direction from Nineveh? | Spain remains his preferred candidate, because Jonah books passage by ship and the Mediterranean trade currents make that western route plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5405" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:05</a> | How long would a full trip around the Mediterranean have taken? | About a month once a ship was in the current stream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:29</a> | Could Ophir have been in Africa since Solomon got apes and different animals? | Possibly. He said apes would most likely point to Africa unless the source were farther east in Asia. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5477" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:17</a> | Would Jonah's storm be the same type as Paul's storm? | Potentially, yes. He connected both to Arctic air flows over the Alps that can create severe Mediterranean storms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5623" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:43</a> | Can ashlar masonry and proto-Aeolic capitals in 10th- and 9th-century Israelite cities suggest kingdom architecture? | Yes in the right context. Capitals alone are not enough, but capitals in palatial architecture strongly suggest kingdom architecture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:40</a> | What was the population of Avaris when it was abandoned? | He cited an estimate of about 29,000 to 34,500 people, roughly 30,000 to 35,000. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=5922" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:42</a> | Does Genesis 30:15 imply Jacob was not giving Leah any of his time? | Yes. He said Leah was getting less time with Jacob and that the mandrakes episode shows Rachel trading a night with Jacob for them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=6321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:21</a> | Did the Semitic settlement at Avaris during the Hyksos era come from outside Egypt like Joseph's family, or from within Egypt? | He said both. In his reading Avaris received more than one wave, including people already in Egypt and people arriving from outside it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=6540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:00</a> | Was Israelite enslavement connected to the downfall of the Hyksos? | Yes. He connected it to the Theban conquest of Avaris and the enslavement of the population at the 17th-to-18th dynasty transition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=6683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:23</a> | How large was Shiloh when it housed the tabernacle, and did it really have northern and southern gates? | He treated Shiloh as a real settlement with enough scale for gate references, likely including northern and southern gates by that stage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=6916" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:16</a> | Why was Shiloh attractive as a base for Israel? | He pointed to its central location in the highlands and likely prior cultic significance, possibly making it ritually and geographically suitable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=7219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:19</a> | Is prophecy meant to be taken conditionally or as determined? | It depends on the prophecy; he says some are conditional, some are determined, and some are recontextualized. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=7314" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:54</a> | Why focus on Bible teaching rather than Islamic debunking and other apologetics content? | He said foundational Bible teaching is more important and more interesting, and that ethnic apologetics requires a specialized, long-term life commitment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=8452" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:52</a> | Can bad ox carts get the driver killed? | He answered jokingly that if a game like Assassin's Creed Origins is any proof, yes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=8475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:15</a> | What should we think about Marian apparitions? Are they satanic or not? | He rejected the either-or framing and said apparitions must be judged by content, especially whether they align with biblical teaching about Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=8803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:43</a> | Are Jesus apparitions, such as Muslims seeing Jesus in dreams, genuine? | He says they are judged by their content; visions that point people back to the biblical Christ are qualitatively different from visions centered elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=9047" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:47</a> | Why did Marian devotion become so prominent in Catholic tradition? | He said medieval Catholicism made Christ and the Father seem too distant and fearful, so Mary became the more approachable figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=9326" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:26</a> | Is Marianism connected to the Isis cult? | He said no; Isis cults had largely died out in the Roman world by the 4th century, while Marianism developed much later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=9468" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:48</a> | Does Marianism come from Gnostic sources? | He was unconvinced and said Gnosticism's hostility to embodiment makes that connection difficult to sustain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=9656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:56</a> | Does Marianism largely postdate Muhammad? | In the West, yes; he said Eastern/Nestorian forms involving Mary predate Muhammad and shaped Muhammad's exposure to Christian ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=9791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:11</a> | Was the Christological debate about two wills or one will the issue that led to a council? | He connected the discussion to Chalcedon and especially Nestorianism, the idea of two persons of Jesus, while also noting other Christological heresies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=10083" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:03</a> | What are a few weaknesses of the late Exodus date? | He said the best early-date pressure point is a prima facie literal reading of 1 Kings 6:1, but that reading must be weighed against Exodus 1:11 and other evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=10518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:18</a> | What solid evidence would make you abandon a late Exodus date? | He said it would take a great deal, because he sees the current evidence as strongly cumulative; any reversal would need substantial archaeological and chronological force. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=10981" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:01</a> | Could the invasion concern in Exodus 1 mean Pharaoh stayed near Avaris because the Israelite problem was rising? | He said the question depends on who the invader is, and that only the Hittites fit the kind of threat needed for his late-date framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=11505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:45</a> | Were the ancient Israelites the only people group to leave a large nation like Egypt? | No. He pointed to large migrations by Philistines and Arameans as examples of major population movements in the ancient Near East. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=11606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:26</a> | Was the prohibition on the way of Horus because of ongoing warfare after the Hyksos expulsion? | He rejected that framing, saying it tries to combine a Hyksos expulsion with a route made dangerous by that same expulsion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=11777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:17</a> | Did the Romans have chariot races, or did the Beach Boys make that up? | The Romans did have chariot races. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=11791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:31</a> | Did the exodus have to follow the Hyksos expulsion by several decades? | Yes. He says the Israelites' enslavement would have to come after the Hyksos defeat and abandonment of the remaining population. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=11887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:07</a> | What are your thoughts on DNA tests such as Ancestry or 23andMe? | He said they can be useful for close relatives but are dangerous for ancestry testing because they expose private family and health data to companies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=12137" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:17</a> | What are key differences between your views and Michael Heiser's views of the Bible and spirit critters? | He said the main difference is contextual material: he works from Late Bronze Age contexts, while Heiser worked more from Second Temple material and the documentary hypothesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=12246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:06</a> | How do you view the divine council? | He views it largely as an Iron Age development and says Bronze Age texts more often show gatherings of gods than a true council advising a high god. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=12461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:41</a> | Are there neutral spiritual beings who are neither for God nor for Satan? | Yes, in his view some chaos monsters fit that category because they are not loyal to either side and simply act according to their own chaotic nature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=12525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:45</a> | Would you say the Council of Gods was a developed theology? | Generally yes. He said that does not mean it is unreal, but it is not a prominent feature of Bronze Age material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=12872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:32</a> | What about ongoing warfare in the Ramesside period along the coast in Exodus 13:17-18? | He said the war would involve Egyptian fortresses on the way of Horus and the Philistine coastal plain, which fits later circumstances better than an early 18th-dynasty setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=13160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:20</a> | Have you read Paradise Lost? | Yes. He said he has read it and finds it entertaining. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=13202" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:02</a> | If Satan has many titles or names, how is he only a type or species of spirit critter? | He said `Satan` is a job title meaning adversary that became a proper name; Satan's species is better described as a cherub. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=13340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:20</a> | Was there any good food in Scotland? | He liked real haggis, white pudding, blood pudding, and Scotch eggs, while saying imitation haggis was not good. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=13413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:33</a> | Did you get a kilt? | No. He said he would want the proper tartan, and in Canada kilts count as formal wear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=13802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:02</a> | Are any alleged relics of saints authentic? | Some may be, but he said there are so many dubious relics that many claims are clearly exaggerated or spurious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=13969" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:49</a> | What is the relationship between modern medicine and divine healing? | He said divine healing is real, but ordinary medicine is also legitimate; miraculous healing tends to appear where need is greatest, not as a rejection of medical care. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=14233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:13</a> | If you were convinced beyond reasonable doubt that the Bible taught an early Exodus, how would you handle that? | He said he would follow the evidence and accept it, because he has no objection to the early date itself if the biblical and contextual evidence supported it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=14467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:01:07</a> | Why do people make such a big deal about the dates of the Exodus? | He tied the dispute to American fundamentalist tradition, literalist tests, and the way some communities made the early date a marker of biblical fidelity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=14727" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:27</a> | Did the late date come from a Scottish scholar, or did the 1906 early-date view come from him? | He said the 1906 early-date view came from the Scottish theologian James Orr, while the late date came from C. R. Lepsius in the 1850s. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=14840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:20</a> | What trustworthy references would help create a beginner class on identifying logical fallacies? | He recommended starting with informal logic, basic syllogisms, and fallacy exceptions, and mentioned Irving Copi's logic text and his own playlist as useful resources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=15480" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:00</a> | Does Acts 15 on blood and meat contradict Paul in 1 Corinthians 8? | No. He read Acts 15 as pastoral boundary-setting for mixed communities and Paul as addressing idolatry and conscience within that same broader framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/C6Kywg71LVY?t=16324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:32:04</a> | Are the Catholic deuterocanonical books worth reading? | Yes, as historical and cultural context, but he said he would not treat them as Scripture. |
